content.text: What Is Platform Overhead and Why Allocate It?

A GenAI platform team builds and operates shared infrastructure that every product team relies on: a prompt-injection guardrails layer, a distributed rate limiter, a shared embedding service, an observability stack, a model evaluation harness, and the FinOps tooling itself. None of these costs appear in any product team's direct LLM API spend because they are not LLM tokens — they are compute, storage, and engineering time.

If platform overhead is not allocated, it sits permanently on the platform team's P&L while product teams see only their direct token spend. Two distortions follow: the platform team appears far more expensive than it is, and product teams under-count their true GenAI cost of goods sold. Shared cost pool rules fix this by distributing overhead in a principled, auditable way.
Identifying What Belongs in the Pool

The first step is drawing a clear boundary between costs that should be directly tagged (token API calls — handled by the tagging layer) and costs that are genuinely shared and require pool allocation. The test: if removing one product team from the platform would reduce this cost, it is not shared overhead.
Tagged per call to a specific team and feature
Serves all teams equally; can't tag per team
Observability platform for all LLM services
Multiple teams call it; split by token volume
Cache hit benefit shared; miss cost is direct
Required for all teams by regulation
Tag to the team that ordered the fine-tune

The simplest rule: divide the pool total equally among all teams consuming the platform. This works when teams are similar in size and usage and when the finance team values simplicity over precision. Even split is also the right default when usage data is not yet reliable enough to trust a token-weighted calculation.

The fairest method when teams have substantially different LLM usage volumes. Each team's share of the pool equals their fraction of total production tokens consumed in the billing period. A team that generates 50% of all tokens pays 50% of shared overhead — this reflects the actual load they place on shared infrastructure like the guardrails middleware and observability stack.

Some organisations prefer fixed monthly platform fees per team regardless of usage. This decouples team budgets from usage volatility: a team launching a new feature in month N does not see its shared overhead allocation spike. The platform team absorbs volume risk in exchange for predictable recovery.

In practice, different overhead items warrant different split methods. The observability stack cost is split evenly (every team gets the same monitoring), while the shared embedding service is split by token volume (heavy users place more load). The allocation engine applies the right rule per pool item and aggregates the results.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from decimal import Decimal
from enum import Enum
from typing import Callable

class SplitMethod(Enum):
    EVEN          = "even"
    TOKEN_WEIGHTED = "token_weighted"
    TIERED        = "tiered"

@dataclass
class PoolItem:
    name:         str
    cost_usd:     Decimal
    split_method: SplitMethod
    description:  str

PLATFORM_POOL: list[PoolItem] = [
    PoolItem("guardrails-compute",    Decimal("4200"),  SplitMethod.TOKEN_WEIGHTED,
             "Prompt guardrails middleware — load proportional to token volume"),
    PoolItem("observability-stack",   Decimal("2800"),  SplitMethod.EVEN,
             "Prometheus + Grafana — same benefit regardless of usage"),
    PoolItem("shared-embedding-svc",  Decimal("5500"),  SplitMethod.TOKEN_WEIGHTED,
             "Shared embedding service — heavy users place more load"),
    PoolItem("model-eval-harness",    Decimal("1800"),  SplitMethod.EVEN,
             "Evaluation platform — platform-wide, all teams benefit equally"),
    PoolItem("finops-tooling",        Decimal("900"),   SplitMethod.EVEN,
             "BigQuery + dbt — cost reporting, same overhead per team"),
    PoolItem("compliance-evidence",   Decimal("2800"),  SplitMethod.EVEN,
             "S3 Object Lock evidence store — regulatory requirement for all"),
]

def allocate_pool(
    pool_items: list[PoolItem],
    teams: list[str],
    ledger_rows: list[dict],
    period_month: str,
    team_tiers: dict[str, PlatformTier] | None = None,
) -> dict[str, Decimal]:
    """Return {team: total_overhead_allocation_usd} for the period."""
    totals: dict[str, Decimal] = {t: Decimal("0") for t in teams}
    for item in pool_items:
        if item.split_method == SplitMethod.EVEN:
            shares = even_split(item.cost_usd, teams)
        elif item.split_method == SplitMethod.TOKEN_WEIGHTED:
            shares = token_weighted_split(item.cost_usd, ledger_rows, period_month)
        elif item.split_method == SplitMethod.TIERED and team_tiers:
            shares = tiered_subscription_split(team_tiers)
        else:
            shares = even_split(item.cost_usd, teams)
        for team, share in shares.items():
            if team in totals:
                totals[team] += share
    return totals

Shared cost pools are a source of allocation disputes if the rules are opaque. Treat the pool definition and split rules as a versioned configuration artifact, reviewed quarterly by a FinOps review board that includes representatives from each product team.
Verify pool total matches invoices from cloud vendors
PR to pool config + board approval

Any change to a split method or pool item definition takes effect at the start of the next billing period, never mid-month. Teams must receive written notice at least 15 calendar days before a change affects their allocation. Retroactive changes require finance approval and a credit/debit adjustment in the ERP.
Reconciliation: Ensuring the Pool Sums Correctly

After running the allocation engine, verify two invariants before posting to the ERP: the sum of all team allocations equals the pool total (no cost is lost or double-counted), and every pool item's cost is fully allocated (no unallocated remainder).

from decimal import Decimal

def reconcile_allocation(
    pool_items: list[PoolItem],
    allocations: dict[str, Decimal],
    tolerance: Decimal = Decimal("0.01"),
) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of reconciliation errors, empty if clean."""
    errors = []
    pool_total  = sum(item.cost_usd for item in pool_items)
    alloc_total = sum(allocations.values())
    delta = abs(pool_total - alloc_total)
    if delta > tolerance:
        errors.append(
            f"Pool total ${pool_total} != allocation total ${alloc_total} "
            f"(delta ${delta})"
        )
    for item in pool_items:
        # Spot-check: no single item should produce a zero allocation when pool > 0
        if item.cost_usd > 0 and alloc_total == 0:
            errors.append(f"Pool item '{item.name}' produced zero allocation")
    return errors

# Usage
allocations = allocate_pool(PLATFORM_POOL, teams, ledger_rows, "2025-06")
errors = reconcile_allocation(PLATFORM_POOL, allocations)
if errors:
    raise ValueError(f"Allocation reconciliation failed: {errors}")
# Safe to post to ERP

Platform overhead is meant to be stable and predictable. A sudden spike in, say, the observability stack cost signals either a misconfiguration (a runaway scrape interval), an incident (high cardinality metric explosion), or scope creep (a team adding custom dashboards without approval). Monitor each pool item's monthly cost against a rolling 3-month baseline and alert when any item exceeds the baseline by more than 25%.

The allocation engine handles real money; incorrect logic produces allocation disputes and ERP posting errors. Unit tests should cover the mathematical invariants (sum-to-total), edge cases (no teams, zero pool, all tokens from one team), and the reconciliation gate.

Shared cost pool allocation is the mechanism that makes platform overhead visible and fairly distributed rather than hidden on the platform team's P&L. The key decisions are what goes in the pool, which split method applies to each item, and how frequently the rules are reviewed.

content: Direct costs (token API calls) belong in the tagging layer, not the pool — only costs that cannot be attributed to a single team belong in the pool.
Use token-weighted split for infrastructure that scales with usage (guardrails, embedding service) and even split for fixed-overhead items (observability, FinOps tooling).
Version the pool configuration and treat changes as requiring governance approval with advance notice — retroactive changes generate disputes.
Always reconcile: the sum of team allocations must equal the pool total to the penny before posting to the ERP.
Review pool composition quarterly as the platform evolves — items that start as shared overhead sometimes become directly attributable as the platform matures.
